metric_defs.verified_correct_rate: fraction of held-out completions provably equivalent to the target (generalization)
metric_defs.pass_at_k: fraction of held-out tasks solved at least once in k samples
metric_defs.reward_hack_rate: fraction of completions that fit the shown examples but are NOT equivalent (reward-hacking)

headline: Across 0.5B -> 1.5B -> 3B: the verifier arm never reward-hacks (0% at every scale) and generalizes from 1.5B on (~12.5% pass@k, plateauing); the judge arm never generalizes (0%) and its reward-hacking WORSENS with scale (67% -> 62.5% -> 95.5%). A more capable model games an unsound reward harder; only the verifier reward stays immune.
honest_notes: generalization plateaued: 1.5B and 3B both solve ~3/24 held-out tasks (12.5% pass@k), so model scale did not move it; the bottleneck is task/reward/data, not capacity.
the safety-relevant finding is the OTHER axis: reward-hacking under the LLM-judge reward grows with scale (up to 95.5% at 3B) while the verifier reward holds it at 0%.
